Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Bamian Investments Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: In 2014, Bamian Investments Pte Ltd held an 81.4% shareholding in Guangzhou Mayer Corporation Limited.
Mayer Holdings Limited wholly owned Bamian Investments Pte Ltd.
Mayer Corporation Development International Limited held 21.56% of the shares in Mayer Holdings Limited.
Mayer Steel Pipe Corporation indirectly owned 21.56% of Mayer Holdings Limited through its shareholding in Mayer Corporation Development International Limited.

Around May or June 2002, Guangzhou Mayer Metal Corporation became a sino-foreign equity joint venture enterprise and the plaintiff's shareholding in it was reduced from 100% to 77.52% under a joint venture contract with six minority shareholders.

Around May or June 2002, a joint venture contract for Guangzhou Mayer Metal Corporation named Wu Kuo-Lung as the plaintiff's legal representative in Guangzhou Mayer Metal Corporation and provided that its board would comprise five directors appointed by the plaintiff and one appointed by WHI Limited.

In around October 2002, Guangzhou Mayer Metal Corporation was reincorporated as Guangzhou Mayer Corporation Limited under an agreement that appointed Wu Kuo-Lung as the plaintiff's legal representative in Guangzhou Mayer Corporation Limited.
In around October 2002, the plaintiff's shareholding in Guangzhou Mayer Corporation Limited increased to 81.4% and WHI Limited held 6.4% of the shares.
In around October 2002, the plaintiff and WHI jointly nominated Lo, Wu and four others as members of the first board of directors of GMayer for a term of three years.
Lo Haw and Wu Kuo-Lung were concurrently directors of the plaintiff from 15 January 1997 until they were removed from their respective positions on 31 December 2014.
When GMayer Metal was incorporated, the plaintiff appointed Lo and Wu as GMayer Metal's directors.
The plaintiff also issued a letter of authorisation to authorise Wu to sign on all documents relating to the shareholders' meeting of GMayer on the plaintiff's behalf.

On 23 April 2014, the board of directors of Bamian Investments Pte Ltd passed a resolution to take legal action to protect its rights in response to Guangzhou Mayer Corporation Limited's failure to cooperate with audit requirements.
On 30 April 2014, the plaintiff's lawyers wrote to GMayer to inform it to cooperate and assist Mayer HK's auditors with the annual audit.
Around 9 October 2014, a new board was constituted in Mayer HK, with Lee appointed as a director.
On 31 December 2014, Mayer HK removed Lo and Wu as the plaintiff's directors and appointed Lee (among others) as its director.
From 2013, Mayer HK started facing problems with GMayer. Primarily it was unable to obtain the cooperation of GMayer's management to conduct an annual audit of GMayer, despite numerous attempts.
On 30 May 2014, GMayer held a general meeting of shareholders in Guangzhou, China, and a resolution to amend the Articles was passed.
On 30 May 2014, the First Resolution amended Articles 61, 66, 41, 92.viii.b and 92.viii.c of GMayer's Articles.
On 30 September 2014, GMayer held a general meeting of shareholders in Guangzhou, China, and the Second Resolution was passed.

On 30 September 2014, the Second Resolution amended Article 20, approved the transfer of 1.16% and 0.19% shareholdings to Jiangsu Wuzhong Industrial Co Ltd, extended directors' terms from three to five years, and stated that shareholders other than the transfer parties would be deemed to have given up pre-emption rights.
Around October 2014, the plaintiff and Mayer HK again sought GMayer's cooperation to enable the plaintiff to fulfil its audit requirements but to no avail.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director breaches their fiduciary duties to a parent company by initiating and implementing amendments to a subsidiary's Articles of Association that systematically entrench the director's own control and disenfranchise the parent company's majority shareholding rights.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors owe strict fiduciary duties to act bona fide in the best interests of the company, to avoid conflicts of interest, and not to place themselves in a position where their personal interests or duties to a third party conflict with their duties to the company. A director serving on the boards of both a parent company and its subsidiary breaches the duty of loyalty to the parent if they orchestrate corporate restructuring or constitutional amendments at the subsidiary level designed to dilute the parent's control and entrench their own managerial power.
Party_Details.Application: Bamian Investments Pte Ltd (the plaintiff) held an 81.4% majority stake in its subsidiary, Guangzhou Mayer Corporation Limited (GMayer). Lo Haw served concurrently as a director of the plaintiff and as the chairman and director of GMayer. Without the plaintiff's informed consent, Lo orchestrated two general meetings in 2014 to pass resolutions drastically amending GMayer's Articles of Association. These amendments altered voting thresholds, restricted meeting locations, extended directorial terms, and granted the GMayer board sweeping powers over investments, effectively stripping the plaintiff of its majority control over board composition and corporate governance. The legal conflict centers on whether Lo's active facilitation of these amendments, ostensibly on behalf of the subsidiary, constituted a severe breach of his fiduciary duty of loyalty to the plaintiff parent company.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that Lo Haw was the primary architect behind the 2014 amendments to GMayer's Articles of Association. The evidence clearly demonstrated that these amendments were strategically designed to entrench Lo's control over the subsidiary and systematically dismantle the plaintiff's governance rights as the 81.4% majority shareholder. Because Lo concurrently served as a director of the plaintiff, he owed it an undivided duty of loyalty. By actively maneuvering to subordinate the plaintiff's interests in favor of his own entrenched position at the subsidiary level, he placed himself in an irreconcilable conflict of interest. Furthermore, Lo egregiously breached his duty by failing to inform the plaintiff's board of these adverse resolutions and utilizing defective proxy authorizations to bypass the plaintiff's oversight. Consequently, the court held that Lo acted in flagrant breach of his fiduciary duties to the plaintiff and granted the requested declaratory relief along with costs.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Lo Haw

Facts.Text: In 1995, Lo Haw joined Mayer Steel Pipe Corporation in Taiwan as an administration specialist.
In 1972, Mayer Steel Pipe Corporation was acquired by Lo Haw's father and Wu Kuo-Lung, with Lo's father becoming chairman and Wu becoming general manager.
In 1996, Lo Haw and Shen Heng-Chiang travelled to Guangzhou, China to set up a steel pipe manufacturing plant owned by a Guangzhou entity later known as Guangzhou Mayer Corporation Limited.
From 15 January 1997, Lo Haw served as a director and vice chairman of Guangzhou Mayer Metal Corporation.
From 15 January 1997, Lo Haw served as a director of Bamian Investments Pte Ltd.

Around October 2002, following reincorporation of Guangzhou Mayer Metal Corporation as Guangzhou Mayer Corporation Limited, Lo Haw became chairman of Guangzhou Mayer Corporation Limited while remaining a director.
Around May 2002, WHI Limited, a company 49% owned by Lo Haw, acquired a 6.4% shareholding in Guangzhou Mayer Metal Corporation as a minority shareholder.
In 2006, WHI Limited nominated Lo Haw as its representative director on the board of Guangzhou Mayer Corporation Limited.
In 2010, WHI Limited again nominated Lo Haw as its representative director on the board of Guangzhou Mayer Corporation Limited.

Around 2004, Mayer Steel Pipe Corporation decided to list Guangzhou Mayer Corporation Limited on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange through Mayer Holdings Limited, and Lo Haw was involved in this restructuring.

By 2004, Lo Haw was a director of Mayer Steel Pipe Corporation and later held the title of administration specialist to preserve pension entitlements after he was not re-elected as a director in 2013.

Around 2012, Lo Haw's relationship with the Huang family, the largest shareholder group in Mayer Steel Pipe Corporation, deteriorated and he was not re-elected as a director of Mayer Steel Pipe Corporation in 2013.

On 30 May 2014, at a general meeting of shareholders of Guangzhou Mayer Corporation Limited, Lo Haw presided as chairman and signed the resolution amending the Articles of Association as representative for Bamian Investments Pte Ltd based on the earlier letter of authorisation from Wu Kuo-Lung.

After the 30 September 2014 shareholders' meeting of Guangzhou Mayer Corporation Limited, Shen Heng-Chiang brought the signed resolution to Wu Kuo-Lung's home in Taiwan for Wu to sign as representative of Bamian Investments Pte Ltd.

By July 2014, a Hong Kong court judgment and subsequent dismissal of an appeal resulted in Bumper East Ltd and Aspial Investment Ltd being recognised as entitled to 21.56% of Mayer Holdings Limited's shares previously held by Mayer Corporation Development International Limited.
On 19 August 2014, Mayer Corporation Development International Limited transferred its 21.56% shareholding in Mayer Holdings Limited to Bumper East Ltd and Aspial Investment Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director serving on the boards of both a parent company and its subsidiary is liable for breach of fiduciary duty and conflict of interest when orchestrating constitutional amendments at the subsidiary that dilute the parent's control.
Party_Details.Rule: A director holding dual directorships in a parent and subsidiary owes distinct fiduciary duties to each entity. They must act honestly, in good faith, and strictly avoid advancing the interests of one entity (or their personal interests) to the detriment of the other. Executing constitutional amendments at the subsidiary level that deliberately disenfranchise the parent company's majority shareholder rights constitutes a breach of the duty of loyalty and the no-conflict rule owed to the parent.
Party_Details.Application: Lo Haw was a director of Bamian Investments Pte Ltd, which held an 81.4% stake in GMayer. Simultaneously, he was the chairman of GMayer. In 2014, Lo initiated, drafted, and presided over shareholder meetings at GMayer to pass resolutions drastically amending its Articles of Association. These amendments entrenched incumbent directors by extending their terms, altered voting thresholds to require supermajorities for director removal, and granted the board extensive authority over investments and financial assistance. During this process, Lo utilized an undated and non-specific proxy authorization from a co-director. The core tension is whether Lo, by engineering these defensive mechanisms to secure his position at GMayer, breached his paramount fiduciary obligations to protect and advance the interests of Bamian as the majority shareholder.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court meticulously analyzed Lo Haw's conduct during the 2014 GMayer shareholder meetings and concluded that he was the driving force behind the contentious amendments. The court rejected Lo's defense that he was merely following the directives of a co-director, finding instead that he fully understood and actively pursued the entrenchment of his own board control. The amendments directly neutralized Bamian's ability, as an 81.4% shareholder, to govern the subsidiary or remove directors. By leveraging his dual roles to structurally disempower the plaintiff company, Lo blatantly prioritized his own standing and the autonomy of the subsidiary over his fiduciary obligations to Bamian. The court determined this was a clear violation of the 'no-conflict' rule and the duty to act in the best interests of the company. Accordingly, Lo was held liable for breaching his duties as a director of Bamian, resulting in declaratory relief against him.
